content.text:
Before developing analytical solution techniques, we explore a powerful 
graphical approach. Even when we cannot solve an equation explicitly, we 
can visualize its solutions.

Consider a first-order differential equation $y' = f(x,y)$. At any point 
$(x,y)$ in the plane, this equation tells us the *slope* of the 
solution curve passing through that point.

content.title: Direction Field (Slope Field)
content.content:
The **direction field** (or **slope field**) of the equation 
$y' = f(x,y)$ is a graphical representation showing short line segments 
at each point $(x,y)$ with slope $f(x,y)$.

items: Choose a grid of points in the $xy$-plane.
At each point $(x,y)$, compute the slope $m = f(x,y)$.
Draw a short line segment with that slope centered at $(x,y)$.

content.text:
Solution curves are functions $y = \phi(x)$ that are tangent to the 
direction field at every point.

content.title: Direction field for $y' = y$
content.problem:
For $y' = y$, the slope at $(x,y)$ equals $y$. Thus:

- On the $x$-axis ($y = 0$): slope is 0 (horizontal segments).

- Above the $x$-axis ($y > 0$): slopes are positive.

- Below the $x$-axis ($y < 0$): slopes are negative.

- Slopes increase as $|y|$ increases.
The solution curves are $y = Ce^x$, exponentials that grow or decay 
depending on the sign of $C$.

content.title: Isocline
content.content: An **isocline** is a curve along which the slope $f(x,y)$ is constant.

content.text:
Isoclines help us draw direction fields efficiently. For $y' = f(x,y)$, 
the isocline where the slope equals $c$ is the curve $f(x,y) = c$.

content.title: Isoclines for $y' = x + y$
content.problem:
The isoclines satisfy $x + y = c$, or $y = -x + c$. These are parallel 
lines with slope $-1$. On the line $y = -x$, all slopes are 0. On 
$y = -x + 1$, all slopes are 1. And so on.

content.text:
Once we have a direction field (or isoclines), we can sketch approximate 
solution curves by following the flow:

items: Start at an initial point $(x_0, y_0)$.
Draw a curve that is tangent to the direction field at each point.
Continue in both directions until you leave the region of interest.

content.text:
This graphical approach provides qualitative insight even when analytical 
solutions are difficult or impossible to find.
